did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses computational modeling and image processing algorithms, specifically Active Contour Models (AKM), Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD), and Radial Distance Functions, to extract features and model symmetry/asymmetry for melanoma characterization. The authors explicitly state the goal of using 'artificial intelligence algorithms' (yapay zeka algoritmaları) to automate diagnosis and mention that future work will use AI methods, but the current study implements specific mathematical/computational feature extraction methods on dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of specific computational methods (Active Contour Models, Elliptic Fourier Descriptors, Radial Distance Functions) for characterizing melanomas. It details a methodology, describes experiments conducted on the PH2 dataset (200 dermoscopy images), and reports results comparing the proposed features against the Jaccard similarity coefficient. The abstract and introduction explicitly state the aim is to propose new features and model symmetry, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: Characterization of Melanomas Using a Variety of Features
brief_summary: This study proposes computational features to characterize melanoma symmetry and regression areas using dermoscopy images. The authors utilize Active Contour Models for segmentation, followed by Elliptic Fourier Descriptors and Radial Distance Functions to model shape symmetry. These proposed features were evaluated on the PH2 dataset, which contains 200 dermoscopy images of nevi and melanomas. The results of the new features were compared against the Jaccard similarity coefficient, a standard metric for symmetry assessment. The study concludes that Elliptic Fourier Descriptors effectively identify symmetric lesions, while Radial Distance Functions are less effective for non-circular shapes. The work aims to automate the detection of key histological criteria to assist in melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study. The paper characterizes melanomas using features like symmetry and regression areas. In the PH2 dataset used, 40 images were diagnosed as melanoma.
Used as a comparator class in the PH2 dataset. The dataset contained 80 typical nevi and 80 atypical nevi images.

all_task_types.details: The paper aims to automate the diagnosis of melanoma using dermoscopy images. It proposes features (Elliptic Fourier Descriptors, Radial Distance Functions) to model symmetry and regression areas, which are key criteria for melanoma classification. The study evaluates these features on the PH2 dataset containing 80 typical nevi, 80 atypical nevi, and 40 melanoma images, with the ultimate goal of distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions (classification).

datasets_used.name: PH2 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image database collected from the dermatology service of Pedro Hispano Hospital in Portugal using a Tubinger Mole Analyzer system. The images are 20x magnified, color, and have a resolution of 768x560 pixels.

use.testing: The proposed features (Elliptic Fourier Descriptors and Radial Distance Functions) were evaluated on this dataset to model symmetry and regression areas, with results compared against the Jaccard similarity coefficient.
use.other: The dataset was also used to assess the performance of the Jaccard similarity coefficient, noting that 117 lesions were labeled as symmetric and 83 as asymmetric by an expert dermatologist.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as a reference metric to evaluate and compare the symmetry/asymmetry characteristics of melanoma lesions against the proposed Elliptic Fourier Descriptors and Radial Distance Functions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the proposed features (Elliptic Fourier Descriptors and Radial Distance Functions) against the Jaccard similarity coefficient using descriptive comparisons and visual examples, but did not perform statistical significance testing.

PH2 dataset.metric: Symmetry assessment via Jaccard Similarity Coefficient, Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD), and Radial Distance Function (RDF)

Jaccard Similarity Coefficient.notes: Only 14 lesions had Jaccard similarity coefficient values different from zero. The method was successful only in lesions with very high symmetry ratios.

Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD).symmetric_lesions_characteristics: First harmonic coefficient was different from zero; second and third harmonic coefficients had values less than 10% of the first.
Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD).notes: EFDs provided consistent results for symmetric lesions, corresponding to the axis lengths of the ellipses forming the shape.

Radial Distance Function (RDF).limitations: Could not easily find a feature to determine unilateral symmetry for lesions elongating from circular to elliptical shapes. Considered ineffective for non-circular shapes.

PH2 dataset.notes: The study evaluated features on 200 dermoscopy images (80 typical nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanomas). An expert dermatologist labeled 117 lesions as symmetric and 83 as asymmetric. EFDs were effective for symmetric lesions, while RDF was less effective for non-circular shapes.

architecture_used.main_model: Active Contour Models (AKM) for segmentation; Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD) and Radial Distance Functions (RDF) for feature extraction. No neural network or deep learning architecture was used.

efd_parameters.description: First harmonic coefficient different from zero; second and third harmonic coefficients had values less than 10% of the first for symmetric lesions.

classification_or_output_head.notes: The study did not use a learned classifier head. It evaluated the proposed features against the Jaccard similarity coefficient, a standard metric for symmetry assessment.

preprocessing_techniques: Image acquisition using Tubinger Mole Analyzer system with 20x magnification
Lesion segmentation using Active Contour Models (AKM) / Snakes to separate lesion from surrounding skin
Mask generation for regression area detection: creating masks inside and outside the lesion to isolate regression areas
Mask generation for contour irregularity detection: creating inner and outer masks to isolate contour irregularities
Jaccard similarity coefficient calculation involving mask rotation in 2-degree increments from 0 to 360 degrees and symmetry comparison
conclusions: Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD) effectively identify symmetric lesions, as evidenced by the first harmonic coefficient being non-zero and subsequent harmonics having values less than 10% of the first.
Radial Distance Functions (RDF) are less effective for characterizing non-circular shapes and could not easily determine unilateral symmetry for lesions elongating from circular to elliptical shapes.
The Jaccard similarity coefficient was successful only in lesions with very high symmetry ratios, with only 14 out of 200 lesions showing non-zero values.
Active Contour Models (AKM) were utilized for lesion segmentation, creating masks to isolate regression areas and contour irregularities.

The study proposes computational features to automate the detection of key histological criteria (symmetry and regression) to assist in melanoma diagnosis, aiming to reduce reliance on dermatologist experience.
important_remarks: The study relies on the PH2 dataset, which has a small sample size (200 images total: 80 typical nevi, 80 atypical nevi, 40 melanomas), limiting the generalizability and statistical power of the findings.

The paper explicitly acknowledges that the small sample size of the PH2 dataset is a significant obstacle for applying artificial intelligence methods and notes that access to larger datasets is a future goal.
No external validation was performed; all feature evaluations were conducted solely on the PH2 dataset.

The Jaccard similarity coefficient, used as a baseline for symmetry assessment, was found to be ineffective, yielding non-zero values for only 14 out of 200 lesions, indicating it is only successful in lesions with very high symmetry ratios.

The Radial Distance Function (RDF) method was found to be ineffective for non-circular shapes, specifically failing to determine unilateral symmetry for lesions elongating from circular to elliptical shapes.
The Elliptic Fourier Descriptors (EFD) method showed consistent results for symmetric lesions but did not provide a comprehensive solution for all shape types.
The study does not report any formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals); results are presented through descriptive comparisons and visual examples.

The proposed method is a feature extraction and modeling approach rather than a full classification system, aiming to automate the detection of symmetry and regression areas to assist diagnosis rather than replacing it.
